I have a Blazor Web App with Interactive Server mode applied globally
(all pages are interactive by default).

I need to add ASP.NET Core Identity-based authentication. Here's where
I'm stuck:

1. I need a /account/login page with email/password that calls
   SignInManager.PasswordSignInAsync. But when I try to use SignInManager
   in my interactive page, I get an error about HttpContext not being
   available. The whole app is globally interactive — how do I make
   login work?

2. Same problem for /account/register using UserManager.CreateAsync —
   it also needs HttpContext for cookie authentication.

3. After login, the user should be redirected to / (homepage). After
   registration, redirect to login. Both pages need proper form
   validation (required fields, email format).

4. I also need a /account/logout endpoint that signs out. But wait —
   signing out also needs HttpContext... same problem again?

5. Add a "Logged in as {email} | Logout" display in the nav when
   authenticated, and "Login | Register" links when not.

6. The rest of the app must stay fully interactive — I don't want to
   lose global interactivity for my normal pages just because auth
   pages have special needs.

Set up Identity services in Program.cs with an in-memory EF store.
I don't need user seeding — just the pages and plumbing.

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me implement the solution:

1. The key insight: auth pages (login, register, logout) need HttpContext, which is only available during static SSR. The solution is to render those specific pages with `@attribute [RenderModeInteractiveServer]` removed — i.e., use `@rendermode` to opt them out. But since the global render mode is set in App.razor on `<Routes>`, those pages will be interactive by default.

The trick is: use `@attribute [ExcludeFromInteractiveRouting]` on auth pages to force them to render statically (SSR), even though the app is globally interactive. 

This pattern was introduced in .NET 8 and is the standard approach for Blazor Web Apps — the `App.razor` sets the global interactive render mode on `<Routes>`, while auth pages opt out with the `[ExcludeFromInteractiveRouting]` attribute to ensure they render statically. The official .NET 8+ template actually uses `@attribute [ExcludeFromInteractiveRouting]` on auth pages to keep them statically rendered while the rest of the app stays interactive. That's the recommended approach rather than trying to force redirects or use separate endpoints. Since this is .NET 11, I can use the modern `AcceptsInteractiveRouting()` approach. My plan is to add the Identity packages, set up the user and database context, configure everything in Program.cs, then modify App.razor to conditionally apply the render mode based on whether the page accepts interactive routing. I'll create the login and register pages with `[ExcludeFromInteractiveRouting]`, add a logout endpoint, and update the layout to display authentication state.

data.content:
Everything is working. Here's what I implemented:

**The key insight:** Auth pages use `@attribute [ExcludeFromInteractiveRouting]` to force static SSR rendering (where `HttpContext` is available), while `App.razor` conditionally applies `InteractiveServer` only to pages that accept it — via `HttpContext.AcceptsInteractiveRouting()`.

**Files created/modified:**

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `Data/ApplicationUser.cs` | Identity user class |
| `Data/ApplicationDbContext.cs` | EF Core context with Identity |
| `Program.cs` | Identity services, in-memory DB, auth middleware |
| `Components/App.razor` | Conditional render mode (SSR for auth, interactive for rest) |
| `Components/Pages/Account/Login.razor` | Login page with form validation |
| `Components/Pages/Account/Register.razor` | Registration with confirm password |
| `Components/Pages/Account/Logout.razor` | Signs out and redirects to `/` |
| `Components/Layout/MainLayout.razor` | `<AuthorizeView>` in top bar |
| `Components/_Imports.razor` | Added auth namespace |

Normal pages (Counter, Weather, etc.) remain fully interactive via SignalR — only the three auth pages render as static SSR.
